topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Dr. Albright was departing. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming beneath my ribs. I stared at the screen, the words blurring slightly. All the late nights spent poring over her feedback, the tentative breakthroughs we’d shared—it all felt suddenly precarious, about to be dismantled. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I walked to the window, watching the campus lights flicker, feeling a surge of bitter resentment towards the entire department, towards the system that dictated these abrupt departures. I needed to prove I didn't need her, that my work stood on its own merit, regardless of her presence.

[story 2]

"So, she's really going?" I managed, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. My voice came out flat, devoid of any warmth. I leaned back in my chair, deliberately straightening my posture until I felt rigid, every muscle tense. I picked up my notes, the paper feeling flimsy in my hands. Every line of research, every carefully constructed argument, suddenly seemed tainted by this impending void. I started aggressively highlighting sections, underlining passages with unnecessary force, as if trying to etch a defiance onto the page. I didn't look up when the departmental announcement finished; I just focused on the physical act of marking, a silent declaration of independence.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug was suddenly too hot, scalding the back of my hand. I paced the length of my cramped office, my steps quick and uneven. Each footfall seemed to jar the silence, a drumbeat of frustration. When I finally sat down, I didn't open my research documents. Instead, I began meticulously cleaning my desk, wiping down surfaces with obsessive focus, moving with sharp, jerky motions. My jaw was tight, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the wood. I kept replaying past conversations in my head, twisting the tone of every exchange until it felt sharp and accusatory, a defense mechanism hardening around my core.
